introduction: Chronic Myeloid Leukaemia (CML), also known as Chronic Myelogenous Leukaemia or Chronic Granulocytic Leukaemia, is a clonal disease resulting from acquired genetic change in a pluripotential haematopoietic stem cell. Altered stem cell proliferation generates a population of differentiated cells, and a greatly expanded total myeloid mass. It is rare below the age of 20 years but occurs in all age groups. There is an increased risk of developing CML with exposure to high doses of irradiation. It is a biphasic or triphasic disease, usually diagnosed in the initial "chronic" or stable phase.

features: Unexplained haemorrhage at various sites e.g. gums, intestinal/urinary tracts

goals: Induce remission to achieve complete remission
drug: Hydroxycarbamide (hydroxyurea) - Adult: 20-30 mg/kg orally daily or 80 mg/kg every third day
Interferon alpha - Adult: 9 million units subcutaneously or intravenously thrice weekly for 6 - 12 months
Imatinib mesylate - 400 mg orally daily (to be used strictly under specialist supervision)
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Profound nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea and abdominal discomfort
Steroids: Cushing's syndrome, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, immunosuppression, infections
All are contraindicated in patients with history of hypersensitivity reactions to the respective medicines
